era_context: Continuation of the early Neo-Assyrian expansion under Ashur-dan II. Egypt remains under the 22nd Dynasty (Libyan). The Levantine city-states are entering a period of increased pressure from Assyrian expansion. Greece continues in the Early Iron Age. The Mediterranean is witnessing the expansion of Phoenician trade networks.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (Chinese sources for this period are unreliable)

events.title: Continuation of Ashur-dan II's campaigns against Aramean tribes
events.region: Mesopotamia and the Levant (Assyria, Syria, Upper Mesopotamia)
events.description: Ashur-dan II continued his military campaigns against Aramean and other tribal groups in the region north and west of Assyria. These campaigns were part of a systematic effort to expand Assyrian control and extract tribute from neighboring territories. The campaigns established Assyrian military dominance in the region and set the stage for the later, more extensive conquests of the 9th century BCE. The exact targets and outcomes of individual campaigns in 933 BCE are not clearly distinguished in surviving sources.

sources.name: Ashur-dan II's royal inscriptions (cuneiform tablets and palace reliefs)

events.certainty_note: Ashur-dan II's reign and general military activity are well-attested, but specific events in individual years are often not precisely dated in surviving sources. The year 933 BCE is assigned by chronological convention within his reign (934–912 BCE).
cross_references: Part of broader Assyrian expansion (934–912 BCE)
Precedes more detailed campaigns of Adad-nirari II

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are clearly attributed to 933 BCE in surviving sources. The Assyrian king lists and inscriptions provide reign dates and general descriptions of campaigns, but do not typically record events on a year-by-year basis for this period. Any assignment of specific military actions to 933 BCE is speculative and based on chronological interpolation rather than direct evidence.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 933 BCE is extremely thin. Ashur-dan II's reign is well-documented in broad strokes, but year-by-year events are not recorded. The surviving inscriptions focus on major campaigns and achievements, not on routine military activity. This year may have seen continued military operations, but without specific contemporary documentation, we cannot identify particular events. The reliance on king lists and later compilations introduces uncertainty about the precise dating of individual campaigns.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of the military consolidation begun in 934 BCE
